original_abstract: This study examines the constitutional debate surrounding the mechanism for electing regional heads in Indonesia, specifically interpreting the phrase "democratically elected" as stipulated in Article 18, paragraph (4) of the 1945 Constitution. Following the constitutional amendments, controversy has emerged between two primary legal interpretations: the first advocating for direct elections by the people to ensure local sovereignty, and the second supporting indirect elections conducted by Regional Representative Councils (DPRD). Through a qualitative analysis of constitutional texts and historical records of the founding fathers' intentions, this research clarifies that local executive elections are legally distinct from the general election regime (Pemilu) used for national legislative and presidential positions. The findings suggest that electing regional heads through the DPRD remains a democratic and legitimate expression of people's sovereignty. Such a representative model aligns with the principle of Pancasila democracy—prioritizing deliberation and consensus—as originally envisioned by the nation's founders, providing a culturally grounded alternative to Western liberal democratic models.
humanized_abstract: Indonesian constitutional scholars have extensively discussed the political system of the country's provincial heads, or governor, through the politics of constitutional interpretation that has seen the Indonesian Constitutional Court juggle between competing legal views on what is means to be 'democratically elected' as provided under Article 18, paragraph (4) of the 1945 Constitution of Indonesia. After the reformation of the Indonesian Constitution in 1999, the democratic procedures of Indonesian provincial heads have been fiercely contested between two opposing views of democracy. Proponents of what might be described as direct democracy advocate voting provincial heads by direct votes from citizens in every province so that regional autonomy and local democracies are effective and democratic. On the other hand, another faction of democracy advocate voting for provincial heads through the houses of people's representatives in the Indonesian Regional Representative Councils. Although the study has undertaken a qualitative jurisprudential approach that scrutinised constitutional history of the constituent assembly and discussions of the Indonesian founding fathers regarding the politics of voting provincial heads, constitutional scholars and Constitutional Courts have failed to take into account sufficiently the legal difference between the voting provincial heads versus voting members to parliament and to the presidency. The conclusion reached by this study is that that voting for provincial heads indirectly via the council of people's representatives has been done democratically elected. Indonesia has fifty-four provinces more than enough multiplicity to make a precedent to argue for diversity in voting a provincial governor and deputy, by taking a look at the deliberations of the constituent assembly that has sought to persuade and accommodate those variants of votes that reflect the plural varieties of democracy as conceived by the founding fathers such as deliberative and consensual democracy in line with principles of Pancasila that are anchored in civilisations rather than a liberalist Western ideology.
